locked_facts: Inserting a dielectric increases capacitance by factor K: C = KC₀
The dielectric reduces the electric field inside by factor K: E = E₀/K
Polarisation of dielectric creates opposing surface charges that reduce the field
With battery connected: V stays same, Q increases, U increases
With battery disconnected: Q stays same, V decreases, U decreases
The dielectric constant K is always ≥ 1 (K = 1 for vacuum)
Energy stored: U = Q²/2C — with dielectric, C increases so U changes depending on boundary conditions
truth_statements: A dielectric is a non-conducting material that when placed in an electric field gets polarised and reduces the net field
The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor with a dielectric filling the gap is C = Kε₀A/d
When a dielectric slab is inserted with the battery connected the charge on the capacitor increases while the voltage remains unchanged
When a dielectric slab is inserted with the battery disconnected the voltage decreases while the charge remains unchanged
The bound surface charges on the dielectric create a field opposing the external field thereby reducing the net field inside

aha_moment.what_to_show: Split view: LEFT = battery connected (V same, Q increases, plates glow brighter), RIGHT = battery disconnected (Q same, V drops, voltmeter reading falls). The dielectric slab slides in and both scenarios animate simultaneously.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Simultaneous comparison of battery-connected vs battery-disconnected with animated charge and voltage changes
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students apply the same rule to both cases — seeing both side-by-side with different quantities changing makes the boundary condition distinction unforgettable

simulation_states.label: Empty capacitor
simulation_states.description: Parallel plate capacitor with vacuum between plates. Field lines shown uniformly between plates. C₀ = ε₀A/d displayed.

simulation_states.label: Dielectric slides in
simulation_states.description: A dielectric slab slides between the plates. Molecular dipoles align, showing polarisation. Bound surface charges appear. Net field reduces to E₀/K.
simulation_states.emphasis: Polarisation creates opposing charges → field reduced inside dielectric

simulation_states.label: Battery ON vs OFF
simulation_states.description: Split comparison: battery connected (V constant, Q increases to KQ₀) vs battery disconnected (Q constant, V drops to V₀/K). Energy bars show opposite trends.
simulation_states.emphasis: Same dielectric, different boundary conditions, different outcomes

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts K value and toggles battery connection. All quantities (C, Q, V, U, E) update in real time. Partial insertion slider shows gradual transition.
simulation_states.emphasis: Change K and battery state — see which quantities change and which stay fixed

common_misconceptions.belief: Inserting a dielectric always increases the stored energy
common_misconceptions.correction: With battery disconnected, inserting dielectric DECREASES stored energy (U = Q²/2C, C increases so U decreases). With battery connected, energy increases because Q increases.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show energy bar decreasing when dielectric inserted with battery disconnected

common_misconceptions.belief: The dielectric creates charge — that's why Q increases
common_misconceptions.correction: The dielectric doesn't create free charge. When battery is connected, additional charge flows FROM the battery because V must stay constant and C increased. The dielectric only polarises.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Animate charge flowing from battery terminals when dielectric is inserted with battery connected
